metadata.title: Pharmacokinetic Profile of NVX-0228 in Preclinical Models

chunks.content: NVX-0228, a selective BRD4-BD1 inhibitor currently in Phase I clinical development, was evaluated in comprehensive preclinical pharmacokinetic studies across multiple species. Our refined biochemical assays determined an IC50 of 8.5 nM against BRD4-BD1, placing NVX-0228 among the most potent BET bromodomain inhibitors reported to date. The compound (MW = 487.3 g/mol, LogP = 2.8) exhibits favorable absorption, distribution, metabolism, and excretion (ADME) properties suitable for oral dosing in humans.

BET bromodomain inhibitors have shown clinical promise in multiple oncology indications, but their development has been hampered by pharmacokinetic challenges including variable oral bioavailability and rapid hepatic clearance. NVX-0228 was optimized for improved metabolic stability through introduction of a fluorine substituent at the C-6 position of the triazolopyridazine core, which reduced oxidative metabolism by CYP3A4. The present study reports the comprehensive preclinical PK characterization of NVX-0228 across mouse, rat, dog, and cynomolgus monkey models.

Biochemical potency was reassessed using a validated TR-FRET assay with full-length BRD4 protein. Under these optimized conditions, NVX-0228 demonstrated an IC50 of 8.5 nM (95% CI: 6.2-11.7 nM) against BRD4-BD1, with selectivity of approximately 85-fold over BD2 (BD2 IC50 = 722 nM). The potency was consistent across three independent assay runs performed on separate days. We note that prior reports using truncated BRD4 constructs may have yielded slightly different IC50 values due to differences in protein preparation and assay format.

Following oral administration at 10 mg/kg in cynomolgus monkeys (n=6), NVX-0228 was rapidly absorbed with a Tmax of 1.2 hours and a mean Cmax of 2,340 ng/mL. The terminal elimination half-life was 6.2 hours (range: 4.8-7.9 hours), and the oral bioavailability was 68% (range: 52-81%) relative to intravenous dosing. The compound exhibited moderate plasma protein binding (92.3% bound) and a steady-state volume of distribution of 3.8 L/kg, indicating extensive tissue distribution.

Metabolic profiling in human liver microsomes identified three primary metabolites: M1 (N-demethylation, 42% of total metabolites), M2 (morpholine ring oxidation, 31%), and M3 (triazole hydroxylation, 18%). CYP3A4 was the predominant enzyme responsible for NVX-0228 metabolism (>80% contribution), with minor contributions from CYP2D6 (12%) and CYP2C9 (5%). In vitro DDI assessment indicated that NVX-0228 is a moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor (IC50 = 4.2 μM) and a weak P-glycoprotein substrate.

In the 28-day repeat-dose toxicity study in cynomolgus monkeys (n=120 total across all dose groups: 25, 50, 100, and 200 mg/kg/day), the primary dose-limiting finding was thrombocytopenia. At the highest dose of 200 mg/kg/day, dose-limiting thrombocytopenia was observed in 32% of subjects, with platelet nadirs occurring between days 10-14 and recovery to baseline within 7-10 days of treatment cessation. At the 100 mg/kg/day dose, thrombocytopenia was observed in 18% of subjects and was generally Grade 1-2 in severity.

The LogP of NVX-0228 was experimentally determined to be 2.8 using the shake-flask method (n=3 independent determinations, SD = 0.15). This value is in the optimal range for oral bioavailability and is slightly lower than the computationally predicted value of 3.2 reported in the original discovery publication. The discrepancy likely reflects the known tendency of computational methods to overestimate LogP for compounds containing basic nitrogen atoms.

Our comprehensive preclinical characterization of NVX-0228 supports its continued clinical development as an oral BRD4-BD1 selective inhibitor. The favorable PK profile, with a half-life of 6.2 hours and 68% bioavailability, is consistent with twice-daily oral dosing. The refined IC50 of 8.5 nM, determined under optimized assay conditions with full-length protein, provides greater confidence in the compound's biochemical potency. While thrombocytopenia remains the primary safety concern, the BD1-selective profile (85-fold selectivity) may offer a wider therapeutic window than pan-BET inhibitors.
